quote: They guess that he has come on some Alchemical errand

quote: Huygens—a brilliant youth... was at work on a pendulum-clock

quote: Cromwell and his New Model Army.

quote: making war against Cromwell and his New Model Army

quote: pay a call on John Wilkins

quote: Christopher Wren

quote: Robert Boyle

quote: Robert Hooke

quote: pendulum-clock I’d seen on the table of Huygens’s house

quote: already several pages into a new alchemical treatise

quote: already several pages into a new alchemical treatise

note: A precursor to chemistry focused on the transmutation of matter, particularly the quest to turn base metals into gold and find the Philosopher's Stone.

quote: Stourbridge Fair was already audible
subject: Stourbridge Fair

quote: drops out a Fahrenheit thermometer on a string. It is the very latest in temperature measurement technology

subject: Huguenots

quote: all marked it as pre-Huygens technology

note: The curriculum of a self-taught scientist of the era, spanning geometry, heliocentrism, physics, and modern rationalist philosophy.

note: A Dutch city that served as a refuge for English Separatists (Pilgrims) before many of them departed for America on the Mayflower.

subject: James I
note: King of England and Scotland (r. 1603–1625) whose economic policies, including the Cockayne Project to restrict cloth exports, caused significant trade disruption.

subject: John Wilkins

subject: Oliver Cromwell

quote: Monsieur de Montmor’s salon in Paris

note: A private scientific society in Paris (1654–1664) that preceded the French Academy of Sciences.

quote: Mr. Daniel Coxe said that mercury had been found running like water

quote: orgies at Whitehall

quote: Goldsmith’s Note. People started doing this about the time you left for Cambridge.

quote: study Natural Philosophy until, literally, the End of the World

note: The precursor to modern science, involving the study of nature and the physical universe before the specialized fields of physics, chemistry, and biology were established.

quote: with an enclosure from one Robert Hooke

note: The world's oldest national scientific institution, founded in 1660 to promote 'physico-mathematical experimental learning.'

quote: Scottish Covenanters down as far as Newcastle

note: Scottish Presbyterians who resisted Charles I's religious reforms, leading to the Bishops' Wars and their occupation of Northern England.

quote: Catholics rebelling in Ulster
subject: Irish Rebellion of 1641
note: An uprising by Irish Catholics against British Protestant settlers, which fueled anti-Catholic paranoia in London.

quote: Europe twenty-five years into the Thirty Years’ War
subject: Thirty Years' War
note: A devastating series of religious and political conflicts (1618–1648) that reshaped the map of Central Europe.

quote: Spain and Portugal dividing into two separate kingdoms
subject: Portuguese Restoration War
note: The 1640 revolution that ended the Iberian Union and restored Portugal's independence from the Spanish Habsburgs.

quote: the Dutch taking advantage of it to steal Malacca from the Portuguese
subject: Siege of Malacca (1641)
note: A key victory for the Dutch East India Company (VOC) over the Portuguese, securing Dutch control over the strategic Strait of Malacca.

quote: the Universal Character is the Alpha—an opening
subject: Universal Characteristic
note: A theoretical formal language that would express logical, scientific, and metaphysical concepts through a universal set of symbols.

quote: Is this anything like Comenius’s project?
subject: John Amos Comenius
note: A Czech philosopher and educator who proposed 'Pansophism,' a system of universal knowledge and language.

quote: Hartlib, Haak, Kinner, Oldenburg—wanted to do when we conceived the Invisible College
subject: The Invisible College
note: A precursor to the Royal Society, consisting of a network of natural philosophers who exchanged ideas through correspondence.

quote: invitation to be Master of Harvard College back in ’41
subject: Comenius and Harvard
note: A historical legend (though debated) that Comenius was offered the presidency of the young Harvard College in 1641.

quote: reasoning by analogy to Cartesian analysis
subject: René Descartes
note: The French philosopher whose method of systematic doubt and algebraic geometry deeply influenced the scientific revolution.

quote: every time Mr. Hooke peers at something with his Microscope
subject: Robert Hooke
note: Hooke's pioneering work with microscopes led to his 1665 book 'Micrographia,' which revealed the hidden structure of the natural world.

quote: He has taken to calling them ‘cells’
subject: Discovery of Cells
note: Hooke coined the term 'cell' because the plant structures he saw reminded him of the small rooms (cella) inhabited by monks.

quote: all things in Earth and Heaven can be classified into forty different genera
subject: An Essay Towards a Real Character, and a Philosophical Language
note: John Wilkins' 1668 work attempting to create a universal language by categorizing all human knowledge into a hierarchical system.

quote: bogged down in an endless digression on the Ark
subject: Noah's Ark
note: Wilkins famously attempted to calculate the exact dimensions and capacity of Noah's Ark to prove it could scientifically hold all known animal species.

quote: How does that enter into the Philosophical Language?
subject: An Essay Towards a Real Character, and a Philosophical Language
note: John Wilkins' 1668 work attempted to create a universal language where words reflected the nature of the things they represented, organized into a vast taxonomic system.

quote: endless digression on the Ark... Noah must have found a way to fit all of these creatures
subject: Noah's Ark in 17th-century Natural Philosophy
note: 17th-century scholars like Wilkins treated the Ark as a literal engineering and biological challenge, using it to prove that the biblical account was scientifically plausible.

quote: certain Continental savants, of an atheistical bent... certain Jesuits turning it against you
subject: 17th-century Religious and Intellectual Conflict
note: The Royal Society faced pressure from both secular 'atheists' and Catholic Jesuits; the former doubted biblical miracles while the latter often defended Aristotelian science against new experimental methods.

quote: honey from Christopher Wren’s Gothic apiary
subject: Christopher Wren
note: Though famous as the architect of St. Paul's Cathedral, Wren was a founding member of the Royal Society and a polymath who designed scientific instruments, including a transparent beehive.

quote: letters addressed to the Royal Society from Huygens, Leeuwenhoek, Swammerdam, Spinoza
subject: The Republic of Letters
note: The Royal Society acted as a hub for a global network of scholars (the Republic of Letters) including Christiaan Huygens (physics/clocks), Antonie van Leeuwenhoek (microscopy), Jan Swammerdam (biology), and Baruch Spinoza (philosophy).

quote: Charles II... had noticed that ant eggs were bigger than ants
subject: Charles II's interest in science
note: Charles II was a patron of the sciences who maintained his own private laboratory and frequently challenged the Royal Society with observations and queries.

quote: Sounds more like a Sun King allusion, Reverend
subject: Louis XIV (The Sun King)
note: Louis XIV of France adopted the sun as his personal emblem to symbolize his absolute power; an allusion to it in a letter to the English King would be seen as politically charged or sycophantic.

quote: Daniel and Isaac had fled plaguey Cambridge
subject: The Great Plague of London (1665-1666)
note: The outbreak forced the closure of universities like Cambridge, leading Isaac Newton to return to his home at Woolsthorpe, where he made many of his most famous discoveries.

quote: remnants of everything that had passed beneath the microscope
subject: Micrographia
note: Robert Hooke's 1665 book 'Micrographia' was a bestseller that featured detailed copperplate illustrations of objects seen under the microscope for the first time, including ants and lice.

quote: Daniel put the needle on the stand and peered through the Microscope.
subject: Robert Hooke
note: Robert Hooke was a polymath and the Royal Society's Curator of Experiments; his work with microscopes revolutionized 17th-century biology.

quote: I will consult my faithful Mercury.
subject: Quicksilver (Mercury)
note: Mercury was used both as a scientific instrument (thermometers/barometers) and, as noted here, a dangerous medicinal treatment for various ailments.

quote: you may look at my drawings while I satisfy the King’s curiosity.
subject: Micrographia
note: Hooke's 1665 book 'Micrographia' was a bestseller that featured stunningly detailed copperplate illustrations of objects under magnification, including the famous flea.

quote: Wilkins has the stone.
subject: Bladder Stones
note: In the 17th century, 'the stone' (bladder or kidney stones) was a common, excruciating, and often fatal condition, frequently discussed by members of the Royal Society.

quote: Oil of vitriol works
subject: Sulfuric Acid
note: The archaic name for sulfuric acid, a highly corrosive mineral acid used in early chemistry and alchemy.

quote: WORD ARRIVED THAT FERMAT had died
subject: Pierre de Fermat
note: A French lawyer and mathematician who died in 1665; he is best known for Fermat's Last Theorem.

quote: leaving behind a theorem or two that still needed proving.
subject: Fermat's Last Theorem
note: Fermat famously claimed to have a proof for this theorem in a margin, but it remained unproven for over 300 years.

quote: King Philip of Spain died, too, and his son succeeded him; but the new King Carlos II was sickly
subject: Charles II of Spain
note: The last Habsburg ruler of Spain, whose physical and mental disabilities (resulting from generations of inbreeding) created a long-standing European crisis over his eventual succession.

quote: Someone named Lubomirski was staging a rebellion in Poland.
subject: Lubomirski's Rokosz
note: A rebellion (1665–1666) by the Polish nobility against King John II Casimir, led by Jerzy Sebastian Lubomirski.

quote: while he worked on Punishments: PUNISHMENTS CAPITAL ARE THE VARIOUS MANNERS
subject: An Essay Towards a Real Character and a Philosophical Language
note: Daniel is assisting John Wilkins in categorizing all human knowledge for his 'Universal Character,' an ambitious attempt to create a scientific, logical language.

quote: the body lifted up into the Air: STRAPPADO
subject: Strappado
note: A form of torture where the victim's hands are tied behind their back and they are suspended by a rope, often resulting in dislocated shoulders.

quote: trying to think of punishments that he and Wilkins had missed
subject: john-wilkins
note: A founding member of the Royal Society and Bishop of Chester, Wilkins was a polymath known for his work on a universal language and early ideas about space travel.

quote: Hooke was aiming the sparks at a blank sheet of paper.
subject: robert-hooke
note: The Royal Society's Curator of Experiments, Hooke was a brilliant polymath who made significant contributions to microscopy, physics, and architecture.

quote: They examined the paper under the Microscope
subject: Microscopy
note: In the 1660s, the microscope was a revolutionary tool; Hooke's 1665 book 'Micrographia' revealed a previously invisible world to the public for the first time.

quote: You see that the Alchemists’ conception of heat is ludicrous
subject: alchemy
note: The passage contrasts the emerging mechanical philosophy (heat as motion) with traditional alchemical views of heat as a literal element or substance.

quote: Molten earth surrounded by the Cœlestial Æther makes a sphere
subject: Luminiferous Aether
note: A theoretical substance once thought to fill all space, serving as the medium through which light and gravity were believed to propagate.

quote: reckon the speed of light
subject: Speed of Light
note: During this era, scientists like Ole Rømer were first beginning to prove that light travels at a finite speed rather than instantaneously.

quote: NED WARD, The Vertuoso’s Club
subject: Ned Ward
note: A London writer and satirist known for 'The London Spy'; his work often mocked the eccentricities of the 'Virtuosos' of the Royal Society.

quote: vain pursuit of the Philosopher’s Stone
subject: The Philosopher's Stone
note: The legendary alchemical substance capable of turning base metals into gold and granting immortality.

quote: the Plague in London was worse. Eight thousand people died in a week.
subject: plague
note: The Great Plague of London (1665-1666) was the last major epidemic of bubonic plague in England, killing an estimated 100,000 people.

quote: Wilkins had finished the Ark digression and begun to draw up a grammar... for his Philosophical Language.
subject: An Essay Towards a Real Character, and a Philosophical Language
note: Wilkins's 1668 work attempted to create a universal language where words reflected the nature of the things they represented; the 'Ark digression' was a famous section calculating the dimensions of Noah's Ark.

quote: experiments concerning the generation of flies and worms out of decomposing meat... spontaneous.
subject: Spontaneous Generation
note: The long-held belief that living organisms could arise from non-living matter; this was being challenged in the 17th century by experiments like those of Francesco Redi.

quote: the air and the water were filled with an invisible dust of tiny eggs and seeds
subject: Spontaneous Generation
note: The passage refers to the 17th-century debate over 'spontaneous generation' vs. 'preformationism'—the idea that life arises from 'seeds' or 'eggs' rather than decaying matter itself.

quote: coming and going in the midst of the Plague
subject: The Plague
note: Refers to the Great Plague of London (1665-1666), which forced the court and many wealthy citizens to flee to the countryside to avoid infection.

quote: That is his money-scrivener.
subject: Money-scrivener
note: A precursor to modern bankers, scriveners originally wrote legal documents but evolved into financial intermediaries who brokered loans and managed estates for the gentry.

quote: handling notes that serve as money
subject: Goldsmith Banking
note: In the 1660s, London goldsmiths began acting as bankers, issuing 'goldsmiths' notes' which became the earliest form of English banknotes.

quote: Lord Chancellor of England
subject: Lord Chancellor
note: The highest-ranking officer of the crown, responsible for the Great Seal and presiding over the House of Lords; a position of immense political and legal power.

quote: Jamestown was founded, the pilgrims scurried off to Leyden, and work commenced on the King James version of the Bible.
subject: Events of the early 17th Century
note: Comstock lists major milestones from 1607-1611 to establish his age and the historical span of his life.

quote: Gunpowder Plots
subject: Gunpowder Plot
note: The 1605 failed assassination attempt against King James I by Catholic conspirators, which remained a major cultural touchstone for anti-Catholic sentiment.

quote: wounded at the Battle of Newark
subject: Siege of Newark
note: A significant Royalist stronghold during the English Civil War that underwent multiple sieges before surrendering in 1646.

quote: crowned in exile at Scone
subject: Coronation of Charles II at Scone
note: Charles II was crowned King of Scots at Scone in 1651 during the Civil War, years before his restoration to the English throne.

quote: Elizabeth, the Winter Queen
subject: Elizabeth Stuart, Queen of Bohemia
note: The daughter of James I whose brief reign in Bohemia led to the Thirty Years' War; her many descendants formed the basis for the Hanoverian succession.

quote: a possible Dutch invasion
subject: Second Anglo-Dutch War
note: A conflict (1665–1667) fought over global trade routes, occurring simultaneously with the Plague and the Great Fire.

quote: House of Stuart
subject: House of Stuart
note: The royal house that ruled Scotland from 1371 and England from 1603 to 1714, characterized by conflict with Parliament and religious tension.

quote: House of Orange-Nassau
subject: House of Orange-Nassau
note: The princely house of the Netherlands; its members often served as Stadtholders and were closely linked by marriage to the English Stuarts.

quote: House of Stuart
subject: House of Stuart
note: The royal house that reigned over Scotland and later England and Ireland; the passage occurs during the Restoration of this dynasty under Charles II.

quote: House of Orange-Nassau
subject: House of Orange-Nassau
note: The princely house of the Netherlands, often in conflict or complex alliance with the Stuarts, eventually leading to the Williamite accession.

quote: Duke of York
subject: James II
note: The brother of Charles II and Lord High Admiral, who would later reign as James II before being deposed in the Glorious Revolution.

quote: The Dutch and the English were at war.
subject: Second Anglo-Dutch War
note: A conflict (1665–1667) fought largely over control of seas and trade routes, occurring simultaneously with the Great Plague of London.

quote: Anne Hyde—a close relation of John Comstock.
subject: Anne Hyde
note: The first wife of James, Duke of York, and mother of two future queens; her marriage was controversial due to her status as a commoner.

quote: The two Princesses, Mary and Anne
subject: Mary II and Queen Anne
note: The daughters of James II and Anne Hyde; Mary would rule with William of Orange, and Anne would be the last Stuart monarch.

quote: Hooke put Daniel to work mending his Condensing Engine
subject: Robert Hooke's Air Pump
note: Based on the vacuum pump designs of Robert Boyle and Hooke, used to study the properties of air and the necessity of air for combustion and respiration.

quote: air contained some kind of spirit that sustained both fire and life
subject: Discovery of Oxygen
note: References early theories of 'vital air' or 'nitro-aerial spirit' that predated the formal discovery of oxygen by Priestley and Lavoisier.

quote: bought them from Spinoza in Amsterdam
subject: Baruch Spinoza
note: The famous Dutch philosopher who supported himself by grinding high-quality optical lenses.

quote: The moon must have gravity, like the earth
subject: Universal Gravitation
note: Reflects the transition from Aristotelian physics to the Newtonian understanding that gravity applies to celestial bodies as well as terrestrial ones.

quote: Kings most commonly, though strong in legions, are but weak in Arguments... MILTON, PREFACE TO Eikonoklastes
subject: Eikonoklastes
note: A book by John Milton published in 1649, written as a justification for the execution of Charles I and a rebuttal to 'Eikon Basilike,' which portrayed the king as a martyr.

quote: seeing the Duke of York out riding and hunting with his princely friends
subject: James II
note: The younger brother of Charles II, known as the Duke of York before his accession; his Catholicism and belief in the divine right of kings were central to the political tensions of the era.

quote: the Duke’s father’s head had plopped into the basket on the scaffold at the Banqueting House
subject: Charles I
note: The execution of Charles I in 1649 at the Banqueting House in Whitehall was the climax of the English Civil War, marking the only time an English monarch was tried and executed by Parliament.

quote: every Dissident meeting-house in the land
subject: Puritans
note: Dissidents (or Nonconformists) were Protestants who did not adhere to the established Church of England, including Puritans, Quakers, and Baptists.

quote: The tables of the Philosophical Language were finished
subject: John Wilkins
note: Wilkins published 'An Essay towards a Real Character, and a Philosophical Language' in 1668, attempting to create a universal language where words reflected the nature of the things they named.

quote: Wilkins drained all the blood out of a large dog and put it into a smaller dog
subject: The Royal Society
note: Early members of the Royal Society, including Wilkins and Lower, performed pioneering (and often gruesome) blood transfusion experiments on animals in the 1660s.

quote: Hooke built a new kind of clock, using the Microscope to examine some of its tiny parts.
subject: Robert Hooke
note: Hooke was the Royal Society's Curator of Experiments; he made significant advances in horology (clocks) and published 'Micrographia,' the first major book on microscopy.

quote: Sir Robert Moray came to visit, and ground up a bit of the unicorn’s horn
subject: Sir Robert Moray
note: A founder of the Royal Society and a polymath who served as a bridge between the scientific community and King Charles II.

quote: the Real Character? ... That’s the alphabet for writing down the Philosophical Language?
subject: Universal Characteristic
note: The 'Real Character' was a system of symbols designed to represent concepts directly rather than words, a precursor to symbolic logic and modern data classification.

quote: planets are kept in their orbits, not by vortices in the æther, but by the force of gravity
subject: Natural Philosophy
note: This references the transition from the Cartesian 'vortex theory' (planets carried by a fluid medium) to the Newtonian theory of universal gravitation.

quote: planets are kept in their orbits, not by vortices in the æther, but by the force of gravity
subject: vortices in the æther
note: Refers to René Descartes' theory of planetary motion, which posited that space was filled with a fluid (aether) that swirled in vortices, carrying planets along. This was the primary rival to Newton's theory of gravity.

quote: Hooke said, nodding significantly at the well.
subject: robert-hooke
note: Robert Hooke was the Curator of Experiments for the Royal Society; the experiment described here (measuring gravity in a well) is one he actually attempted.

quote: Hooke called out a weight in pounds, ounces, grains, and fractions of grains
subject: Apothecaries' system
note: Before the metric system, English scientists used various systems like Troy or Apothecaries' weight; a 'grain' was a precise unit based on the weight of a seed of cereal.

quote: Cells, spiders’ eyes, unicorns’ horns, compressed and rarefied air
subject: Micrographia
note: These are references to Robert Hooke's 1665 book 'Micrographia,' the first major publication of the Royal Society, which featured famous illustrations of objects seen through a microscope.

quote: philosophical languages
subject: universal-characteristic
note: Refers to the 17th-century quest by Wilkins, Leibniz, and others to create a logical language where symbols directly represented concepts rather than sounds.

quote: John Wilkins
subject: john-wilkins
note: A founding member of the Royal Society and polymath who worked on universal languages and the possibility of travel to the moon.

quote: Isaac was doing up at Woolsthorpe
subject: isaac-newton
note: Woolsthorpe Manor was Newton's family home, where he retreated during the Great Plague (1665-1666) and performed his most famous work on calculus, optics, and gravity.

quote: spirits of wine, dyed with cochineal
subject: cochineal
note: A vivid red dye derived from crushed insects found in the Americas, highly prized and used here to make the fluid in a thermometer visible.

quote: Hooke opened up the thorax of a live dog
subject: Vivisection
note: The practice of performing operations on live animals for scientific research was a common, though controversial, practice of the early Royal Society to understand circulation and respiration.

quote: realized it was the Duke of York in person
subject: james-ii
note: James Stuart, the Duke of York, was the brother of King Charles II and later became King James II.

quote: My brother is fond of your Society... for he made it Royal
subject: charles-ii
note: King Charles II granted the Royal Society its royal charter in 1662, providing it with institutional legitimacy.

quote: Hooke and Wilkins had thrust a brass pipe down the dog’s windpipe
subject: Robert Hooke
note: A brilliant polymath and the Royal Society's Curator of Experiments, known for his work in microscopy and his contentious relationship with Newton.

quote: Hooke and Wilkins had thrust a brass pipe down the dog’s windpipe
subject: John Wilkins
note: A founding member of the Royal Society and Bishop of Chester, he was obsessed with universal languages and scientific cooperation.

quote: James Stuart, Duke of York and Lord High Admiral
subject: James II
note: The younger brother of Charles II, James served as Lord High Admiral and later became King James II; his Catholicism and military background are central to the novel's political tension.

quote: Some are of the opinion that mercury cures it.
subject: Quicksilver (Mercury)
note: Mercury was the standard, albeit highly toxic, treatment for syphilis for centuries, leading to the phrase 'A night with Venus, a lifetime with Mercury.'

quote: Anne Hyde, who had therefore probably given it to the daughters, Mary and Anne.
subject: Anne Hyde
note: The first wife of James II and mother of two future queens (Mary II and Queen Anne); her marriage was controversial due to her non-royal status.

quote: There were plenty of bastards like Monmouth.
subject: Duke of Monmouth
note: The eldest illegitimate son of Charles II, who eventually led a rebellion to claim the throne from his uncle James II.

quote: the son of a Phanatique?
subject: Phanatiques
note: A derogatory Restoration-era term for non-conformist Protestants (like Puritans) who were viewed as religious extremists and political rebels.

quote: refugees from the Black Death
subject: The Plague
note: The Great Plague of London (1665-1666) forced many, including the Royal Society and the King's court, to flee the city for the countryside.

quote: used to study the spontaneous generation of flies.
subject: Spontaneous Generation
note: The then-common (but now debunked) belief that living organisms could arise from non-living matter, such as maggots appearing from rotting meat.

quote: Isaac had sent him a letter
subject: Isaac Newton
note: The legendary physicist and mathematician, depicted here in his early years at Cambridge during the plague years.

quote: harmony between the heavenly orbs and the orbs we view them with
subject: Musica Universalis (Music of the Spheres)
note: An ancient philosophical concept regarding the proportions in the movements of celestial bodies as a form of music; it was a central theme in Johannes Kepler's work on planetary motion.

quote: read about Dr. Waterhouse’s early memories of Newton and Leibniz
subject: Isaac Newton
note: One of the most influential scientists in history, Newton is a central figure in the novel's exploration of the Scientific Revolution.

quote: read about Dr. Waterhouse’s early memories of Newton and Leibniz
subject: Gottfried Wilhelm von Leibniz
note: A German polymath and philosopher who co-invented calculus; his intellectual rivalry with Newton is a major theme of the Cycle.

quote: when he’s shooting the sun with his back-staff
subject: Back-staff
note: A navigational instrument used to measure the altitude of the sun by observing its shadow, allowing the navigator to avoid looking directly at the sun.

quote: Dappa is an African
subject: Dappa
note: Though a fictional character, Dappa represents the presence of educated Africans in the maritime world and the complex social hierarchies of the era.

quote: refuses to have anything to do with pirates
subject: Pirates
note: Piracy was a significant threat to Atlantic trade in the early 18th century, often blurring the lines between criminal activity and state-sponsored privateering.

quote: ‘Twas a grenade. We have a few retired grenadiers in our number
subject: Grenadiers
note: Specialized soldiers in the 17th and 18th centuries trained to throw early gunpowder grenades, often chosen for their height and strength.

quote: thinks about Hooke and Wilkins with their poor dogs
subject: Robert Hooke
note: A brilliant experimental philosopher and curator of experiments for the Royal Society, known for his work in microscopy and physics.

quote: thinks about Hooke and Wilkins with their poor dogs
subject: John Wilkins
note: A founding member of the Royal Society and Bishop of Chester, known for his work on a universal language and early scientific experiments.

quote: Hooke and Wilkins with their poor dogs
subject: Early Vivisection and Respiration Experiments
note: Refers to real 17th-century experiments by the Royal Society involving air pumps and the physiology of breathing, which often used live animals.

quote: Woolsthorpe was as pleasant a specimen of English countryside as he’d ever seen.
subject: Woolsthorpe Manor
note: The family home of Isaac Newton where he returned during the Plague years (1665–1666) and performed his most famous experiments on calculus, optics, and gravitation.

quote: on a blank shield, a pair of human thigh-bones crossed.
subject: Newton Family Coat of Arms
note: The actual historical crest of the Newton family, which Isaac Newton formally submitted to the College of Arms in 1705.

quote: like certain ghastly effigies in Papist churches, with exposed bleeding hearts and gushing stigmata.
subject: Catholic Iconography
note: A reference to the Sacred Heart and other vivid Baroque religious art common in Catholic ('Papist') Europe, which would have seemed grotesque to English Puritans.

quote: participating in Hooke’s dog experiment
subject: Robert Hooke
note: Refers to a famous 1667 Royal Society experiment where Hooke kept a dog alive by using bellows to pump air through its lungs, proving that the motion of the lungs wasn't what sustained life, but the air itself.

quote: to re-encounter anyone, after so long, during the Plague Years
subject: The Plague
note: The Great Plague of London (1665-1666) forced the closure of Cambridge University, sending Newton back to Woolsthorpe.

quote: Daniel swung round Isaac in a wide Copernican arc
subject: Nicolaus Copernicus
note: A metaphorical reference to the Copernican model where the Earth revolves around the Sun, reflecting the shift in scientific thought during this era.

quote: I have been staring into the sun too much.
subject: Newton's Optics
note: Newton actually performed dangerous experiments on his own eyes, including staring at the sun's reflection and poking his eye with a bodkin to observe the effects on his vision.

quote: contradict the theories of Descartes, Boyle, and Huygens
subject: Natural Philosophy
note: Newton's work on light challenged the established 'mechanical' theories of his contemporaries René Descartes, Robert Boyle, and Christiaan Huygens.

quote: I have divided the white light of the Sun into colors, and then recombined these rays
subject: Newton's Prism Experiments
note: Newton's 'Experimentum Crucis' proved that white light is composed of a spectrum of colors, rather than the prism 'coloring' the light as was previously believed.

quote: The naval battle—we are fighting the Dutch in the Narrow Seas.
subject: Second Anglo-Dutch War
note: The conflict (1665–1667) between England and the Dutch Republic over trade routes. The specific battle mentioned is likely the Battle of Lowestoft (June 1665).

quote: James, Duke of York... stood on the deck of a flagship
subject: James II
note: The future King James II, who at this time served as Lord High Admiral and personally commanded the English fleet against the Dutch.

quote: Do you remember when Cromwell died, and Satan’s Wind came along
subject: Death of Oliver Cromwell
note: Cromwell died on September 3, 1658, during an exceptionally violent storm which Royalists claimed was the Devil coming to fetch his soul.

quote: centrifugal force (as Huygens has named it)
subject: Christiaan Huygens
note: A Dutch mathematician and physicist who published the first laws of centrifugal force and was a major contemporary rival/influence to Newton.

quote: I have developed a method of fluxions
subject: The Calculus Priority Dispute
note: Newton's early term for his version of calculus, which he used to calculate the slopes of curves and the changing rates of physical quantities.

quote: Wilkins, who had written a novel in which men flew to the moon
subject: John Wilkins
note: Refers to Wilkins' 1638 work 'The Discovery of a World in the Moone,' which speculated on the possibility of space travel.

quote: imaginary Jesuits hurling Latin barbs at him
subject: Jesuits
note: The Society of Jesus was known for its rigorous scientific education; Newton frequently clashed with Jesuit scientists like Ignace-Gaston Pardies over his theories of light.

quote: Isaac fancied himself as a combination of Galileo and St. Anne
subject: Galileo Galilei
note: The Italian astronomer who was tried by the Inquisition for heliocentrism; Newton views him as a precursor in the struggle for scientific truth.

quote: unlike St. Anne he would not end up riddled with his tormentors’ arrows
subject: Saint Sebastian
note: The text likely intends Saint Sebastian, the martyr famously depicted riddled with arrows; St. Anne is the mother of the Virgin Mary and not associated with this iconography.

quote: Isaac’s bedroom... doing prism experiments
subject: Newton's Optics
note: Newton's 'crucial experiment' (experimentum crucis) using prisms to prove that white light is composed of a spectrum of colors.

quote: light shining from Venus. “Have due patience,” Isaac said... he could now see not only the spectrum from Venus
subject: Newton's Optics
note: Newton's experiments with prisms and light at Woolsthorpe during the plague years (1665-1666) led to his discovery that white light is composed of a spectrum of colors.

quote: JOHN BUNYAN, The Pilgrim’s Progress
subject: The Pilgrim's Progress
note: A famous Christian allegory written by John Bunyan, a Puritan who was imprisoned for his religious beliefs; it is one of the most significant works of English literature.

quote: someone died of the Plague and they had to move out again
subject: The Plague
note: The Great Plague of London (1665-1666) was the last major epidemic of bubonic plague in England, killing an estimated 100,000 people.

quote: Wilkins (now back in London, running regular meetings of the Royal Society again)
subject: John Wilkins
note: A polymath and founding member of the Royal Society who served as its first secretary.

quote: running regular meetings of the Royal Society again
subject: The Royal Society
note: The oldest national scientific institution in the world, granted a royal charter by Charles II in 1660 to promote 'physico-mathematical experimental learning.'

quote: working on the Universal Character manuscript
subject: Universal Characteristic
note: Wilkins's attempt to create a 'philosophical language' that could express all scientific and philosophical concepts through a logical system of symbols.

quote: The Year of the Beast, 1666
subject: Apocalypticism
note: Many in the 17th century believed 1666 was the year of the Apocalypse due to the '666' in the date, a belief fueled by the Plague and the Great Fire.

quote: War continued, and it was more than just an Anglo-Dutch war now
subject: Second Anglo-Dutch War
note: A conflict (1665–1667) between England and the Dutch Republic for control over seas and trade routes, in which France eventually sided with the Dutch.

quote: whatever plans the Duke of York had hatched with his Admirals
subject: James II
note: The Duke of York (later King James II) served as Lord High Admiral during the Anglo-Dutch wars.

quote: Books of Daniel and of Revelation

note: The ancient medical theory that health is governed by the balance of four bodily fluids: blood, phlegm, black bile, and yellow bile.

quote: stranger than anything gawkers would pay to view at St. Bartholomew’s Fair
subject: St. Bartholomew’s Fair
note: A massive annual summer fair in London known for its 'freak shows,' theatrical entertainments, and rowdy atmosphere.
chapter: Aboard Minerva, Cape Cod Bay, Massachusetts

quote: a Dutch flag, and below it, a smaller one depicting the Ægis
subject: The Aegis
note: In Greek mythology, the Aegis is the shield or breastplate of Athena; here it serves as the emblem for the Minerva, named after the Roman equivalent of Athena.
chapter: Aboard Minerva, Cape Cod Bay, Massachusetts

quote: drive them through the hem of each flag into the wood of the mast
subject: Nailing colors to the mast
note: A defiant naval tradition where a captain nails his flag to the mast to signal that the ship will never surrender, as the flag cannot be lowered.
chapter: Aboard Minerva, Cape Cod Bay, Massachusetts

quote: A fifth doctrine, that tendeth to the dissolution of a commonwealth... —HOBBES, Leviathan
subject: Thomas Hobbes
note: An influential English philosopher whose 1651 work 'Leviathan' argued for absolute sovereignty as a means to avoid the 'war of all against all' seen during the English Civil War.

quote: London during this the Third Dutch War, waiting for news of the Fall of Holland.
subject: Third Anglo-Dutch War
note: A conflict (1672–1674) where England and France allied against the Dutch Republic; it was deeply unpopular in England due to religious and economic concerns.

quote: cannonball rolling down a Spiral of Archimedes in a wooden barrel
subject: Archimedean Spiral
note: A mathematical curve described by Archimedes; in the context of the theater, it is used here to create a mechanical sound effect for thunder.

quote: King Charles came frequently to watch his Nellie sing her pretty songs
subject: Nell Gwyn
note: A famous Restoration actress and mistress of King Charles II, known for her wit and popularity with the public.

quote: Wilkins had been the first. Many more followed, as if the Bishop of Chester had launched a fad.
subject: John Wilkins
note: A polymath, founding member of the Royal Society, and Bishop of Chester who died in 1672.

quote: fell under the guns of Admiral de Ruyter at Sole Bay
subject: Battle of Solebay
note: The first naval battle of the Third Anglo-Dutch War (1672), where the Dutch fleet under Michiel de Ruyter surprised the joint English and French fleets.

quote: King Charles shut down the Exchequer one day
subject: Great Stop of the Exchequer
note: In 1672, Charles II suspended payments on government debts, causing a financial crisis and ruining many London goldsmith-bankers.

quote: libels depicting the atrocities of King Looie’s army in Holland
subject: Louis XIV
note: The 'Sun King' of France, whose invasion of the Netherlands in 1672 (the 'Rampjaar' or Disaster Year) sparked the Third Dutch War.

quote: so these held up Goldsmiths’s Notes addressed to Thomas Ham
subject: Goldsmiths' Notes
note: Early forms of paper money and banking; goldsmiths issued these receipts for gold deposited with them, which then circulated as currency.

quote: Just the other day, Hooke figured out how arches work.
subject: Robert Hooke
note: Hooke discovered the principle of the catenary arch (the shape a hanging chain takes), which he expressed as 'As hangs the flexible line, so but inverted will stand the rigid arch.'

quote: Christopher Wren is going to re-design all the arches in St. Paul’s
subject: Christopher Wren
note: The lead architect responsible for rebuilding London's churches after the Great Fire, most notably St. Paul's Cathedral.

quote: shame about the Guinea Company, what?
subject: Company of Royal Adventurers Trading into Africa
note: Also known as the Guinea Company, this was a chartered company set up by the Stuart family to trade in gold, ivory, and slaves; it faced financial ruin during the Anglo-Dutch Wars.

quote: James, Duke of York.
subject: James II
note: The younger brother of Charles II and future King James II, who served as Lord High Admiral and was a controversial Catholic figure.

quote: creating a new Royal Africa Company.
subject: Royal African Company
note: Established in 1672 to replace the failed Guinea Company, it held a monopoly on the English slave trade and was led by the Duke of York.

quote: The Duke of York has resigned the Admiralty…
subject: Test Act of 1673
note: The Test Act required all holders of public office to take communion in the Church of England; James resigned as Lord High Admiral because he had converted to Catholicism.

quote: marry that nice Catholic girl
subject: Mary of Modena
note: The second wife of James, Duke of York; her Catholicism and the prospect of a Catholic heir fueled political unrest in England.

quote: spiritous essence, found in fresh air, for which fire competes with living animals.
subject: Discovery of Oxygen
note: Refers to early experiments by Robert Boyle and Robert Hooke regarding the 'vital' component of air necessary for both combustion and respiration.

quote: naming New York after him?
subject: Capture of New Amsterdam
note: In 1664, the English seized New Amsterdam from the Dutch and renamed it New York in honor of the Duke of York.

quote: St. Paul’s will be rebuilt there anon—’pon mathematick principles
subject: St Paul's Cathedral
note: The medieval cathedral was destroyed in the Great Fire of 1666; Christopher Wren's redesign used innovative geometry and engineering.

quote: Christopher Wren means to do yonder
subject: Christopher Wren
note: The preeminent architect of the era and a founding member of the Royal Society, responsible for rebuilding London's churches.

quote: Wren will use Hooke’s principles
subject: Robert Hooke
note: Hooke collaborated with Wren on the engineering of St. Paul's, particularly the use of the catenary curve for the dome's stability.

quote: King seized the gold and silver... in the Tower of London
subject: Seizure of the Mint (1640)
note: Charles I seized £130,000 of merchants' bullion kept at the Tower of London to fund his wars, destroying trust in the Crown as a safe depository.

quote: practice of goldsmith’s notes, arose as a result
subject: Goldsmith Bankers
note: The precursor to modern paper money; merchants began depositing gold with goldsmiths, who issued 'notes' that circulated as currency.

quote: William of Orange had opened the sluices
subject: The Dutch Water Line
note: During the Franco-Dutch War (1672), the Dutch intentionally flooded their own land to create an impassable barrier against French invasion.

quote: Quakers, Barkers, Ranters, Diggers, Jews, Huguenots, Presbyterians
subject: English Dissenters
note: A list of various radical religious and political sects that emerged or grew during the mid-17th century, often in opposition to the established Church of England.

quote: Those mock-Polish grenadiers were formed up in front of the building that had been the Guinea, and was soon to be the Royal Africa, Company.
subject: Royal African Company
note: A major English mercantile company granted a monopoly on the West African slave trade; it was led by the Duke of York (the future James II).

quote: They tried to get in at Gresham’s College.
subject: Gresham College
note: The original meeting place of the Royal Society in London, which also served as a temporary site for city government after the Great Fire.

quote: eventually reached Bedlam, and found an evening’s refuge there amid piles of dressed stone and splats of mortar.
subject: Bethlem Royal Hospital (Bedlam)
note: The infamous psychiatric hospital; at this time, it was being rebuilt at Moorfields into a grand Baroque structure designed by Robert Hooke.

quote: In other words, he was asked to parrot what Wilkins would’ve said
subject: John Wilkins

quote: as two inert substances, mixed in an Alchemist’s mortar, can create a fulminating compound
subject: Alchemy

quote: Hooke was there, too, for Gresham’s College had become too crowded
subject: Robert Hooke

quote: only went out into the city to meet with Leibniz.
subject: Gottfried Wilhelm von Leibniz

quote: If you and France had conquered the Dutch Republic, you would have much more to be ashamed of
subject: Franco-Dutch War
note: A conflict (1672–1678) where England initially joined France to attack the Dutch Republic, a move that was deeply unpopular with English Protestants.

quote: Oldenburg pesters me every day to complete the Arithmetickal Engine.
subject: Henry Oldenburg

quote: Oldenburg pesters me every day to complete the Arithmetickal Engine.
subject: Computing Machines

quote: When I get back to the Bibliothèque du Roi, I will turn all of my efforts to mathematics.
subject: Bibliothèque du Roi
note: The French Royal Library in Paris, where Leibniz spent significant time researching and where he developed his version of calculus.

quote: The other is the composition of the continuum, or: what is space? Euclid assures us that we can divide any distance in half
subject: Zeno's Paradox

quote: You have already demonstrated, before the Royal Society, that you know as much of those techniques
subject: The Royal Society

quote: in the view of Sir Roger L’Estrange, anyway
subject: Sir Roger L'Estrange
note: An English press censor and staunch Royalist pamphleteer known for his fierce opposition to religious tolerance and political dissent.

quote: If you sum this series, it will slowly converge on pi.
subject: Leibniz formula for pi
note: The infinite series (1 - 1/3 + 1/5 - 1/7...) discovered by Leibniz, which represents a major milestone in the development of calculus.

quote: scratched out the first few terms of a series: “If you sum this series, it will slowly converge on pi."
subject: Gregory's Series
note: Leibniz discovered the infinite series for pi (1 - 1/3 + 1/5 - 1/7...) around 1673. It was a major milestone in early calculus, though it converges very slowly.

quote: My friend Isaac Newton has done wizardly things with them. He has learned to approximate any curve as an infinite series.
subject: Isaac Newton
note: Newton's work on power series and the binomial theorem allowed him to represent complex curves and functions as infinite sums, a cornerstone of his development of calculus.

quote: giving him a way to calculate the tangent to a curve at any point.
subject: The Tangent Problem
note: Finding the tangent to a curve is the fundamental problem of differential calculus. Both Newton and Leibniz developed independent methods to solve this.

quote: From time to time, Oldenburg or Comstock or I cajole him into writing out some of his mathematical work.
subject: Henry Oldenburg
note: As Secretary of the Royal Society, Oldenburg acted as a central hub for scientific correspondence, often pressuring secretive scientists like Newton to publish their findings.

quote: Huygens can cajole you, when you get back.
subject: Christiaan Huygens
note: A preeminent Dutch mathematician and physicist who mentored Leibniz in Paris, introducing him to higher mathematics and the work of Pascal and Descartes.

quote: I shall endeavour to have an Arithmetickal Engine built in Paris, to satisfy Oldenburg
subject: Leibniz's Stepped Reckoner
note: Leibniz designed one of the first mechanical calculators capable of all four arithmetic operations, improving upon Pascal's earlier design.

quote: No one told me to think about free will versus predestination. I plunged into the middle of the labyrinth
subject: The Labyrinth of the Continuum
note: Leibniz famously described two 'labyrinths' of the human mind: the first concerning free will and providence, and the second concerning the composition of the continuum (the nature of infinity and atoms).

quote: JOHN BUNYAN, The Pilgrim’s Progress
subject: The Pilgrim's Progress
note: A 1678 Christian allegory by John Bunyan, written while he was imprisoned for nonconformity; it is one of the most significant works of English literature and central to Puritan culture.

quote: Dawn-light had flooded the turret of Bedlam
subject: Bethlem Royal Hospital (Bedlam)
note: The infamous London psychiatric hospital. Robert Hooke was the architect of its new building at Moorfields, completed in 1676.

quote: the well-conceived, precisely executed maneuvers of birds, and of Robert Hooke.
subject: Robert Hooke
note: The Royal Society's Curator of Experiments, known for his polymathic brilliance, irritability, and his work in microscopy, architecture, and mechanics.

quote: panes of glass, neatly scored with black Cartesian grids, plotted with foreshortened parabolae—the trajectories of actual cannonballs.
subject: Ballistics
note: The study of projectile motion was a major focus of 17th-century science, moving from Aristotelian theories to the parabolic paths identified by Galileo.

quote: He was paying attention to part of a rarefying engine: one of many such piston-and-cylinder devices he and Boyle used
subject: The Air Pump
note: Robert Boyle and Robert Hooke developed the air pump to create vacuums, leading to the discovery of Boyle's Law regarding gas pressure and volume.

quote: Such powder may be unsuitable for the Rarefying Engine
subject: Rarefying Engine
note: A term used in the 17th century for an air pump, a device Robert Hooke and Robert Boyle developed to create a vacuum for scientific experimentation.

quote: magazine of one of His Majesty’s Ships of War
subject: Naval Gunpowder Management
note: The transition from loose powder to pre-measured cartridges (bags) was a significant safety and efficiency innovation in 17th-century naval warfare.

quote: I have harbored such notions from time to time about Oldenburg
subject: Henry Oldenburg
note: The first Secretary of the Royal Society; he and Hooke had a notoriously litigious and paranoid relationship regarding intellectual property.

quote: Your friend Newton shows signs of the same affliction.
subject: Isaac Newton
note: Newton was famously prone to bouts of paranoia and social withdrawal, often referred to as 'melancholy' or nervous breakdowns.

quote: found it in the magazine of his ship on the eve of their sailing for Sole Bay.
subject: Battle of Solebay
note: The first naval battle of the Third Anglo-Dutch War (1672), which resulted in heavy English casualties and a tactical Dutch victory.

quote: mostly on the bottom of the sea, thanks to Admiral de Ruyter.
subject: Michiel de Ruyter
note: The highly skilled Dutch Admiral who led the Dutch fleet to several major victories against the English and French during this period.

quote: Someone other than the King and the Duke of York.
subject: James II
note: The Duke of York (later James II) served as Lord High Admiral and commanded the fleet at the Battle of Solebay.

quote: Like a Screw of Archimedes.
subject: Archimedes' Screw
note: A machine historically used for transferring water; Hooke is here adapting the principle for aerodynamics/propulsion.

quote: Now that Anglesey has been elected President of the Royal Society
subject: The Royal Society
note: The premier scientific institution of the era; the presidency was a position of significant political and scientific influence.

quote: Only the queer churches of Dutchmen and Jews and Dissenters.
subject: Dissenters
note: A broad term for English Protestants (like Puritans, Quakers, and Baptists) who refused to conform to the Church of England.

quote: Roger Comstock appeared at the gates of Bedlam
subject: Bethlem Royal Hospital (Bedlam)
note: London's notorious psychiatric hospital; in this period, it was a public institution where the wealthy often visited to gawk at the inmates.

quote: escort you to Whitehall... the King wants you there for the signing.
subject: Whitehall Palace
note: The main residence of the English monarchs in London from 1530 until 1698.

quote: Why, the Declaration! Haven’t you heard? Freedom of conscience for Dissenters
subject: Royal Declaration of Indulgence (1672)
note: Charles II's attempt to extend religious liberty to Protestant nonconformists and Roman Catholics, which was highly controversial in Parliament.

quote: just as Wilkins wanted it.
subject: John Wilkins
note: A polymath and Bishop of Chester who advocated for 'latitudinarianism'—a more inclusive and tolerant Church of England.

quote: a muffled curse from Hooke
subject: Robert Hooke
note: A brilliant polymath and Curator of Experiments for the Royal Society, known for his work in microscopy and his contentious relationship with Newton.

quote: For a leading Dissident, it is entirely proper
subject: English Dissenters
note: Protestants who separated from the Church of England; they faced various legal restrictions and were often associated with specific political and social circles.

quote: following Roger and the valet into Bedlam
subject: Bethlem Royal Hospital (Bedlam)
note: London's notorious psychiatric hospital; in this period, it was a common landmark and sometimes a site where the public could pay to view the inmates.

quote: powder-bag for the Navy... now that the war is over
subject: Third Anglo-Dutch War
note: A conflict (1672–1674) between England and the Dutch Republic; the passage refers to the aftermath and the political fallout regarding naval readiness.

quote: since we first studied together at Trinity
subject: Trinity College, Cambridge
note: The prestigious college where Isaac Newton resided and where much of the early scientific and academic development in the novel takes place.

quote: smuggled into the ‘Siege of Maestricht,’ where it caused a cannon to explode
subject: Siege of Maastricht (1673)
note: A key battle in the Franco-Dutch War where the Duke of Monmouth led English troops; the novel uses it as a setting for a specific act of sabotage.

quote: found their way into David Jones’s Magazine
subject: Davy Jones's Locker
note: An idiom for the bottom of the sea; Roger is euphemistically stating that the powder bags were lost at sea or sunk.

quote: since the Plague Year, Daniel, at every meeting of the Royal Society
subject: Great Plague of London
note: The last major epidemic of the bubonic plague in England (1665-1666), which forced many characters to flee London for the countryside.

quote: The ability to do them’s what separates me from Nell Gwyn.
subject: Nell Gwyn
note: A famous Restoration actress and one of the most well-known royal mistresses of King Charles II, often cited as a 'rags-to-riches' figure of the era.

quote: Ten inches of sheepgut with a knot in one end
subject: Early Contraception
note: A reference to early condoms made from animal intestines (sheepgut), which were used in the 17th century primarily to prevent the spread of syphilis.

quote: the coach rattled into the middle of St. Bartholomew’s Fair
subject: St. Bartholomew's Fair
note: A massive, often rowdy annual summer fair in Smithfield, London, known for its mix of commerce, entertainment, and 'freak shows' or curiosities.

quote: during an attaque of the bloody phlux
subject: Dysentery (The Bloody Flux)
note: A common and often fatal period term for dysentery, characterized by severe diarrhea with blood.

quote: serving as their London seat since the Wars of the Roses
subject: Wars of the Roses
note: A series of 15th-century dynastic civil wars for the throne of England, used here to emphasize the ancient lineage of the 'Silver' Comstocks.

quote: he had been spending so much time with Leibniz
subject: Gottfried Wilhelm von Leibniz
note: The German polymath and philosopher whose views on the 'mind of God' and the nature of time/space are being reflected in Daniel's observation of the painting.

quote: That man was a Cavalier. He led cavalry charges against the Parliamentarian foot-soldiers.
subject: The English Civil War
note: The conflict between 'Cavaliers' (Royalists supporting Charles I) and 'Roundheads' (Parliamentarians), which Roger uses to contrast the old aristocracy with the new 'Golden' generation.

quote: watched the King sign the Declaration. As penned by Wilkins
subject: Royal Declaration of Indulgence (1672)
note: Charles II's attempt to extend religious liberty to Protestant nonconformists and Roman Catholics, which was politically controversial and later rescinded.

quote: As penned by Wilkins, this document had given freedom of conscience to everyone.
subject: John Wilkins
note: A polymath, founder of the Royal Society, and Bishop of Chester who sought to reconcile different religious factions through 'latitude' and reason.

quote: outlawed certain extreme heretics, such as Arians who didn’t believe in the Trinity.
subject: Arianism
note: A theological belief that denied the divinity of Jesus Christ; in this era, it was considered a dangerous heresy and was a secret belief held by some scientists, including Isaac Newton.

quote: sausage-casings in a bedchamber... ten inches of knotted sheepgut
subject: Early Condoms
note: In the 17th century, 'sausage-casings' or 'sheaths' made of animal intestines (sheep or goat) were used primarily to prevent venereal disease and, as seen here, to navigate religious prohibitions against 'touching'.

quote: men of your religion to say so
subject: Puritans
note: The passage refers to the strict moral and sexual codes of Puritans and the casuistry some used to justify sexual acts that didn't technically involve skin-to-skin contact.

quote: I am an actress... playing the role of mistress
subject: Restoration Theater
note: The Restoration era was the first time women were legally allowed to perform on the English stage; actresses often occupied a precarious social position between celebrities and courtesans.

quote: a leading Dissident
subject: English Dissenters
note: Also known as Nonconformists, these were Protestants (like Puritans) who separated from the Church of England following the Act of Uniformity 1662.

quote: a Fellow of the Royal Society
subject: The Royal Society
note: The preeminent scientific institution of the era, founded in 1660 to promote 'Physico-Mathematical Experimental Learning'.

quote: being a Natural Philosopher
subject: Natural Philosophy
note: The precursor to modern science, involving the study of nature and the physical universe before the specialized fields of physics or biology existed.

quote: compete against Isaac
subject: Isaac Newton
note: One of the most influential scientists in history, whose work on gravity, motion, and optics defined the era's scientific revolution.

quote: Leibniz
subject: Gottfried Wilhelm von Leibniz
note: A German polymath and philosopher who co-invented calculus independently of Newton.

quote: Hooke
subject: Robert Hooke
note: The Royal Society's Curator of Experiments, known for his work in microscopy and his contentious relationship with Newton.

quote: as Calculus would’ve been to Mayflower Ham
subject: Calculus
note: The mathematical study of continuous change, which was a cutting-edge and highly complex discovery during the late 17th century.

quote: he is not willing to believe, yet, that he is controlled by his balance of humours.
subject: Humorism
note: The ancient medical theory that human health and temperament were governed by the balance of four bodily fluids (blood, phlegm, yellow bile, and black bile).

quote: BLACKBEARD IS AFTER HIM!
subject: Edward Teach (Blackbeard)
note: A notorious English pirate who operated around the West Indies and the eastern coast of Britain's North American colonies.
chapter: Aboard Minerva, Cape Cod Bay, Massachusetts

quote: The water is like the Cœlestial Æther, a fluid medium through which all things move.
subject: Luminiferous Aether
note: A postulated medium in early modern physics thought to fill all space and allow for the propagation of light and gravity.
chapter: Aboard Minerva, Cape Cod Bay, Massachusetts

quote: It’s all in the arithmetic—the same sort of arithmetic that Flamsteed, the Astronomer Royal
subject: John Flamsteed
note: The first Astronomer Royal of England, known for his meticulous star catalogs and his professional rivalry with Isaac Newton.
chapter: Aboard Minerva, Cape Cod Bay, Massachusetts

quote: at the Observatory in Greenwich
subject: Royal Observatory, Greenwich
note: Commissioned in 1675 by King Charles II, it played a major role in the history of astronomy and navigation.
chapter: Aboard Minerva, Cape Cod Bay, Massachusetts

quote: proving that Sir Isaac’s latest calculation of the orbit of the moon is wrong.
subject: Isaac Newton
note: The preeminent physicist and mathematician whose theory of universal gravitation attempted to explain the complex motion of the moon.
chapter: Aboard Minerva, Cape Cod Bay, Massachusetts

quote: turning Dappa’s steady observations into sines and cosines, conic sections and fluxions.
subject: Fluxions
note: Newton's specific term and notation for what is now known as differential calculus.
chapter: Aboard Minerva, Cape Cod Bay, Massachusetts

quote: Teach has too much intelligence for that, and so breaks the other way
subject: Edward Teach (Blackbeard)
note: The notorious pirate known as Blackbeard, who operated in the West Indies and the eastern coast of Britain's North American colonies.
chapter: Aboard Minerva, Cape Cod Bay, Massachusetts

quote: one of Minerva’s carronnades manages to carry away one of the sloop’s sails
subject: Carronade
note: A short, smoothbore iron cannon used by the Royal Navy; it was powerful at short range but lacked the distance of long guns.
chapter: Aboard Minerva, Cape Cod Bay, Massachusetts

quote: The men who’ve been manning the guns are told to make like Cincinnatus
subject: Lucius Quinctius Cincinnatus
note: A Roman statesman and military leader who became a legendary symbol of civic virtue by resigning his near-absolute power immediately after a crisis to return to his farm.
chapter: Aboard Minerva, Cape Cod Bay, Massachusetts

quote: swinging wide the studdingsail booms
subject: Studdingsails
note: Extra sails used in light winds to extend the width of the square sails, significantly increasing a ship's speed when sailing downwind.
chapter: Aboard Minerva, Cape Cod Bay, Massachusetts

quote: everyone knows that Queen Anne’s Revenge is a bit crowded
subject: Queen Anne's Revenge
note: The flagship of the pirate Blackbeard, originally a French slave ship named La Concorde captured in 1717.
chapter: Aboard Minerva, Cape Cod Bay, Massachusetts

quote: she begins the passage to the Old World sailing large before a quartering wind
subject: Sailing Large
note: A nautical term for sailing with the wind blowing from behind the widest part of the ship, allowing for maximum speed and efficiency.
